title: In 1986, two Florida journalists tried to buy crack for a news story and nearly got killed
author: Ellsworth Toohey
summary: In 1986, Rudy Giuliani and Senator Al D'Amato staged a crack buy in Washington Heights for the TV cameras. Giuliani's disguise was a Hell's Angels leather vest bearing "Dirty Thirty" and "Filthy Few" patches, which he wore over business attire. D'Amato threw on an ARMY cap but kept his tasseled loafers. —
full_text:
In 1986, Rudy Giuliani and Senator Al D'Amato staged a crack buy in Washington Heights for the TV cameras. Giuliani's disguise was a Hell's Angels leather vest bearing "Dirty Thirty" and "Filthy Few" patches, which he wore over business attire. D'Amato threw on an ARMY cap but kept his tasseled loafers. A fleet of federal agents had their backs, but the two still looked like attorneys who'd wandered off from a tailgate.

Kipp Friedman, a young reporter at the Boynton Beach News Journal in South Florida, watched the footage and figured he could do the same thing. Without the federal agents. He and photographer Paul Milette put on jeans and plaid shirts, grabbed $20, hid a camera in Paul's black Chevy pickup, and drove into Boynton Beach's drug corridors near an elementary school, Friedman writes in a new memoir essay on Substack.

A school custodian spotted them immediately: "Say, what you two white boys doin' in that truck? You the po-lice? Wanna get shot!" They parked near a convenience store anyway. Within minutes, teens and young adults swarmed the truck, waving baggies and shouting their inventory: "Yo! Base Rock! Crack! Blow! Weed!"

Then someone spotted the camera gear. A fist-sized limestone rock came through the rear window, passed between their heads, cracked the front windshield, and landed on the dashboard. They floored it. Another car blocked them, Paul swerved onto the sidewalk, and a man with a gold tooth leveled something that looked very much like a gun at them.

They made it to the Boynton Beach police station. Captain Carl Dixon heard the story and offered his assessment: "Man! You shoulda' bought what they was sellin'."

The banner art for Friedman's Substack (shown here) was created by illustrator Rob Sussman, whose work has appeared in the Nation, the New York Times, and more.
